sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient presents with anxiety regarding her recent blood work, which revealed a positive HCV antibody test and an elevated AST.
She reports being surprised by the diagnosis, as she has been feeling fine.
She mentions a history of IV drug use 15 years ago and reports a decreased use of alcohol and cigarettes.
She also mentions feeling tired at the end of the day, but attributes it to work and not getting enough sleep.
She has a family history of hypertension, diabetes, and depression.
Past medical history: The patient reports a history of IV drug use 15 years ago and a history of smoking, currently down to one to two cigarettes a day.
She reports a history of alcohol use, currently drinking about a beer a day, with occasional increase on weekends.
Review of systems: The patient denies any other symptoms besides fatigue at the end of the day.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient appears in no distress and no jaundice on skin exam.
The heart exam reveals a regular rate and rhythm with a 2/6 systolic murmur, which has not changed from last year.
The lung exam is clear with no wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.
The abdominal exam is normal, with present bowel sounds, a soft abdomen, and no hepatosplenomegaly.
The musculoskeletal exam is normal, with no gait disturbance and no edema.
Test Results: The patient's recent blood work shows a positive HCV antibody test and an elevated AST of 39, but normal liver panel results for albumin and total bilirubin.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient is diagnosed with hepatitis C, a viral infection that affects the liver, which she likely has had for years.
Problem list: Hepatitis C; active; confirmed.
Plan:
I will order additional blood work, including checking the HCV RNA and HCV genotype, to confirm the diagnosis and determine the severity of liver disease.
I will also order an ultrasound to assess liver fibrosis.
I will schedule follow-up appointments for the patient's family members to screen for HCV.
Follow-up: I will see the patient in about three weeks to review the results of the additional testing and determine the next steps in treatment.
